speaker: interviewer
text: December 21, 1995. Tape number three. My name is Daniel Sedlis. I am interviewing Ira Kaminsky. You were talking about 1,000 people from Plotzk--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who were brought to Chmielnik ghetto. This was in April of '41 when the ghetto was established?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was established April '41. When they Plotzk came-- the people from Plotzk came was a little later. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was the end of '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship? How did-- you said it became very crowded. How did the people from Chmielnik and the people from Plotzk get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got along very, very OK. But they came without anything, the people from Plotzk. And like I said before, the cro-- we were so crowded. If anybody had two rooms, they had to give up one room, depends upon how many children they had. And we squeezed somehow. As a matter of fact, I was at that time in the committee for relocating the Plotzk. They were-- in the beginning, they were-- we kept them in shul, in the synagogue. Then slowly, a little by little, we located every one. And, and I have to say about the people of Chmielnik, they did share as much as possible with the people, with the-- with the refugees that we had from many-- from many, many, from many places.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other refugees did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Radom they came also. From the, the surrounding towns they came. They concentrated--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have refugees living in your home at any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I lived-- yes, I lived in a home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you said you were on the committee to relocate the refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They t-- they took-- you know, they wanted to know. So they assigned for various sections. You had to relocate about 200 people and make a list of who lives so and so. They had already a list.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Judenrat. How many people are living in one or two, two rooms. And we just went in, and we tried, you know, to explain to the people. Listen, there is a war, and this is a order. We have to receive these people. They are now refugees. You don't know if you can-- if you'll become tomorrow a refugee. We have to share with them as much as possible the quotas that we have for now. Thank god everybody was relocated. Better, worse, you know. But everybody had a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the cultural life like in the Chmielnik ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cultural life? Number one, there was no schools for the children. My little brothers and sisters, there were no school. What I did as much as possible, we taught them a little Polish, Hebrew. As a matter of fact with Hebrew, I would like to be bring ones for teaching. One of our teachers, a religious teacher, Lacks, was killed because he was teaching young people. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Lacks. Meyer Lacks. He was my teacher also. How do you spell the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-A-K-X it was, what spells that. And, and you know, it was terrible. You know, the children were growing without any, any learning, without learning anything. Anyway, we did something ourselves to teach the children. But the problem was with Hebrew. There was no-- they didn't allow any Hebrew schools. Though I, I have to bring it up. I had a good friend, and I went with him to school-- to Polish school-- about seven years. And he was also going a half a day to Polish school and a half a day to yeshiva. And he was very intelligent, a fine, intelligent-- my, my best friend. We used to make our homework in our house. And he once organized-- came to my brother, little brother Mendel, and if he wants to learn. He said, of course I want to learn. So but on the condition you can't tell anybody. Nobody could know that you are learned. I will teach you. You will learn [NON-ENGLISH]. You will learn Russian and all these things. But you cannot, under no circumstances, nobody has to know that you are learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once my brother came, my little brother, my young brother Mendel came and he said, Israel-- so they called me-- I would like you should for [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. What, what it means, you should go and listen to what I have to check and see what I learn in the Bible. I said, you want I should check on you? Where did you learn? I can't tell you that, he said, but I learned. And what do you say? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took out the Bible, the parashah [? was ?] Vayikra. And he said the parashah. I was so amazed. And this was thanks to my good friend, [? Altob ?] Schenzer. That I brought it-- I wrote about it. And this man was a real-- he jeopardized his own life for teaching young children like my brother and others.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that it was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, I found out. I had to tell him. Because he was not afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told them, only your father after he'll check what you learned, you can tell them who the teacher is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so Mendel--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me-- Mendel told me, you know who the teacher is? Your best friend [? Altob ?] Schenzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told his brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me that [? Altob ?] Schenzer was the-- then I saw [? Altob ?] Schenzer and I thanked him very much but watch out nobody should catch you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he survived even though he did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't survive, I'm sorry to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he did not?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't survive. He went to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not because of the teaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- no. Not because of the teaching because he picked-- he didn't just pick anybody. He picked people that he was sure that they wouldn't squeal on him to nobody, that they will keep a secret that he is the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people continued to study religion. Was there other cultural life? Theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry to say, no. We were not allowed to congregate at all. But people, whatever they had, is in the evenings we were not allowed to go out because from 5 o'clock till 8 o'clock in the morning, no Jew was allowed to leave his house. So congregating only from one neighbor to the other, we could go, but not to cross streets, that the Germans shouldn't find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was the major force that was occupying Chmielnik? Was it German or Polish collaborators? Who was the major presence?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had Gendarmerie, you know. We had also Jewish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish police, nobody feared. They were all our friends before that. Most of them were fine boys. They behaved nice. Sometimes I heard later on that there were a couple of them that didn't behave as much. You know, they said, it was their duty, they have an order to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they selected to be on the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were selected-- mostly they, they picked fine boys from before the war. They were very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who picked?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat selected them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat picked them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they became the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all watching in the Jewish neighborhood in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who the head of the police was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the head of the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pasternak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pasternak.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was really a man, an actor. And he was an intelligent, fine man. And he did his job. But I was told that after the liquidation of Chmielnik, he didn't behave as good as it was expected from him. Some people had a grudge against him. But personally--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have any personal--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not personal. Because no, I didn't have anything. I stayed away from the police. Once I was arrested for not going to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By, by the Jewish police. And they put me into-- I was only one day over there. It was a mistake, and everything else. They let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were-- in the police station or was there a prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- it was the police station, not the Jewish police. It was the general police. As a matter of fact, I was sitting with Polish thieves that time. There were three of them, the thieves. One of them was working for my-- for my uncle, and they behaved very nice with at night. They shared even cigarettes with me. And that's all I, I, I had. Then we came closer in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let me ask one more question. And that is was there a resistance movement that you were aware of in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A resistance-- we started to organize. The Polish organized. We could not compare with the Polish. Number one, they were free-- free to go from one city to the other. A Jew was not allowed to go out from, from the ghetto even. They had-- they had weapons from before the war. They were hiding some of the weapons. The Jewish people didn't-- they didn't have any weapons. They went to the army. They went to the army. But we didn't. We had ones before the war-- a gun, a Betar party that I used to belong. But where, who had it, where it wind up, I don't know who had it. The resistance-- I had a steady contact with the Polish resistance. Not everybody, not every Jew. I had a very good friend, a Polish man, who went to school with me together. We played together Polish theater. And we became friends. And he was active in the Polish underground. And I was-- and he supplied all the publications that the underground published at the time illegally. And you know, they, they were-- they were bringing what they are doing, what kind of activities, that they are blowing up trains, that they are-- that they are attacking or you know, the German police, and sabotage ops.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was informed. I used to help. But one thing I will never forget. It was in August, the beginning of September. Beginning of September, I believe it was. It comes after they liquidated Kielce. Kielce was a big city, about 30,000 Jewish people. And I had many families over there. My aunts lived over there, my cousins, All right? And they liquidated Kielce. At that time, they were talking, what happened to them? You know, the Germans said that they are transferring from one place to the other, the people. They didn't say that they are-- that they are killing them. Later on we found out. How did I found out? I had supplied that illegal paper publication from the Polish underground, [POLISH]. And over there they never brought any news, any facts about the Jewish people because this was, a matter of fact, anti-Semitic underground organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that time they brought something and they brought up the first time that I heard and I read that time was that they brought up the name of Treblinka. And they said, the people-- that the Germans are liquidating the ghettos-- are going to Treblinka. And they brought also what happened to them. They are gassed and burned. I was shocked. Right away I took the paper, and I ran to my good friend-- I had many friends from before the war from the same organization, the Zionist organization-- [? Maer ?] [? Feldman. ?] He was a fine, intelligent, and active member of the Zionist movement. And I showed it to him. You know, the man said, I don't believe it. I can't believe it. The Nazis might be terrible murderers, but to take young people, and all the people and gas-- gassing. This was the first time that they brought up about gas. Gassing them, he couldn't believe it. As a matter of fact, he showed me. Here, I'll show you the official policy of the Third Reich of Germany. And he showed me a paper, the official paper of the Third Reich. And they called it The editor was Joseph Goebbels. He was-- he was the propaganda minister of the Third Reich. And he was the editor, and he wrote an editorial. And he says, here, I want you to read that editorial. This is the official policy of the Third Reich. And he wrote that time, he said-- I'm trying to remember what it was over there in the paper. He said, yes, it's true that we don't have-- we are not as rich as the Americans. We don't have what they have. They can-- they can show-- they have-- they have everything, whatever they need to run the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what we have is the power of human-- how did he say it-- of human power, you know? Work-- for human work power. This we have, and we can match the Americans with their richness with our work power that we have. So he showed it to me. You see, here young people like we are. We are working. We are working power, you know? We have to work for nothing for them. How can they-- if they-- he's very proud of it that he's got the work power. He's going to kill the work power, his own-- his own power? This I can't believe. And he didn't believe it. Did you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I, I did believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I saw the, the liquidation of Kielce. Gave me a hint that they are not transferring them to another place. Otherwise we would hear and we would know where they were-- where they were transferred-- where they were transferred.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the fact that you didn't hear about where your relatives from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't hear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know that there was a liquidation in Kielce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. We did hear that the ghetto in Kielce was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: That it no longer existed.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is no longer in existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know what happened to the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't what happens. Since I had the paper--

speaker: interviewer
text: You suspected.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I thought that this is it. And we had an argument. Well, that [? Maer ?] [? Feldman ?] didn't go with us. And he-- as a matter of fact, he went to Treblinka. He later found out. He must have found out they were-- what they are doing with the people in Treblinka. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was the contact that I had with the Polish underground. As a matter of fact, Maer Feldman with other friends, they were collecting money for the Polish underground. As a matter of fact, he didn't know that I am collecting. I didn't know that they are collecting. And I went to one friend, the same that he was asking to buy-- it was 100 zloty one piece of paper, you know, a receipt for the Polish underground. Then he found out, Maer Feldman, and another friend of mine, [? Ian ?] [? Berg, ?] find out that I was also involved with the Polish underground. Involved but we didn't belong to them. We were planning. We were talking about joining a Jewish-- a Jewish branch. And we should join somehow the Polish underground. But that time-- this was in September. [DRINKING] In October 1st-- I mentioned something. They had took-- they made a selection in the marketplace and they took out over a 1,000, close to 1,200 people, Jewish young people-- boys, and girls, men, women. And they took us to a work camp, Skarzysko-Kamienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was October '42, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was Oc-- this was October 1st, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was Skarzysko-Kamienna located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skarzysko-Kamienna was about-- about 50-- about 50 kilometer from Chmielnik. And they there waiting. Well, the Germans were all over. They surrounded our city. Polish police, Ukrainians, and they took us that time to Skarzysko. Five days after, October 6th, when I was already in Skarzysko, they took all the helpless people-- mostly young kids and elderly people and some young people if there are not too many. And they called them to the targoviste, to one place and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: The targoviste is the central area? It's the market?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the targoviste. It was a marketplace where the farmers, the Polish farmers brought their stock, cows, and food, and horses and all other things to sell to the Jewish people. And, and they can't-- they told the Jewish people over that everybody should go over there. And if not, they will-- whoever will not go, will be shot. As a matter of fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know about this? How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told later on. I didn't know what happened. Anyway, October 6th, they liquidated the ghetto in Chmielnik. I was not there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said you found out later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later-- we were in Skarzysko camp. We found out from the Polish people that they liquidated Pol-- this Chmielnik. We were not sure, so we, we paid one Polish man. He should go to Chmielnik, and find out and let us know immediately. We sent out and paid a Polish man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went there, and he came back. He said, the ghetto doesn't exist anymore. All the Jewish people were taken away from Chmielnik. Where? They didn't know. But later on we found out they went to Treblinka gas chambers. And when did you find out about the Treblinka gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the first time from the underground. Later on-- later on, we found out from Warsaw. In Warsaw, they, they took also from Warsaw, every day, a certain amount of people, and they send them away. And the Judenrat wanted to know. First they said that they were relocating them to, to work. Labor camps, they're taking the people. But the sent spies to follow the people where they are going. And that was the time when they found out in Warsaw that they are taking the people to Treblinka. And this was the reason, if I can remember, the chairman of the Judenrat committed suicide. Adam-- I, I forgot right now. His first name was Adam. He committed suicide because he, he didn't want to be part of the liquidating in this way of the Jewish people. And this was the end of Chmielnik. In Skarzysko-Kamienna there was already when we came, there were a lot of Jewish people already over there from various cities. I was transferred. There were three camps really, not one-- A, B, and C. A and B was a bet-- well, camps very bad also. But C was the worst of all three of them. In Werk C, Jewish life wasn't worth it a penny. All the sadists-- it was a haven for the sadists to do whatever they, they wished, whatever they wanted just with the Jewish people-- beating, killing even. In first place, on top were the [NON-ENGLISH,] the Ukrainians. The Ukrainians behaved like animals. They were-- they supposed to watch us around the camp. We were closed in. They supposed to watch nobody should escape from the camp. But instead they were watching-- they were-- they were-- they came to the barracks, and they were looking in through the windows. And if they saw any money-- that somebody's taking out money or they're exchanging any money, they called them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the second day, the third or sec-- second or third fay when we were there is one man from our city. He called them out. Well, he was afraid, you know? He had to go out. When he took-- when he came out, he took away all the money that he had and he killed him on the spot. Later on, he said that he was trying to escape. But it was not true. He just-- they just wanted the money from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the commandant said, even if the [N0N-ENGLISH] are calling you at night, especially don't go out in the side near the gate around. Then they can accuse you of that you are trying to escape. And we were very careful. Of course they killed people, Jewish men. One friend-- one from my city, he was a nice looking man. And they couldn't see-- if somebody was good looking or dressed humanely, they couldn't take it. So they took him out. I remember his name was Limberg. And they took away, of course, everything, whatever he had. They thought that he's rich, that he has some money, and they killed him just for behaving nice, for good looking, and dressed like clean dressed. I was assigned to Werk C like I said. And Werk C was a-- was a camp that if somebody did something wrong--

speaker: interviewer
text: Werk C meaning Camp C?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work C. I will call it-- because we used to call it Werk C.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can call it Werk C. That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: C is in Polish, 'ts.' Werk--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. I understand. Werk C.

speaker: interviewee
text: Werk C. And if somebody did something wrong in Werk A and B, they sent to Werk C. If somebody was accused of, of sabotage or anything, or did something wrong intentionally or unintentionally, or if somebody was already exhausted and sick, they couldn't produce whatever they-- they send them to Werk C to, to be-- to finish them off. In Werk C, we had various types of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why were you Werk C?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just dividing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Chmielnik, there was over 1,000 people. They took about 300, 400 to Werk C, 400 to Werk A, and werk-- to Werk B.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just had bad luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my brother and me were sent to Werk C. My sister, Rochelle, went to Werk A, A, Werk A. And I was standing over with my brother together. He just went between my brother and me. They divided. And I went to work to a different place, and he was a transportation. Transportation. But I was assigned to work in a factory which produced and made underwater mines. The material was a chemical which was a powdery chemical pressed in squares, in a certain squares. It was two by three by five squares, solid squares. And we had to work a norm, a certain amount to produce. And in the beginning when we were still healthy, we could do it hardly. Because they figured out if you can do five a minute, five one minute, and 60 times, five, 300 per hour, and so on and so 2,500, for instance, a day during the eight-- the eight hours of work. And it was very, very hard for us to produce these things. That's what I was assigned. I was-- I was-- OK. I was assigned to work in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
